name: Tempus AI, Inc.

profile.description: Tempus AI is a healthcare technology company that has built one of the world's largest libraries of clinical and molecular data, using artificial intelligence to deliver precision medicine at scale. The company operates across two segments: Diagnostics (genomic profiling, molecular diagnostics, MRD testing) and Data & Applications (AI platform licensing to biopharma companies for drug discovery and development). Founded in 2015 by Eric Lefkofsky after a personal experience with cancer, Tempus IPO'd on NASDAQ in June 2024 at $37/share and reported TTM revenue of approximately $1.27B as of Q1 2026.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): Founded in 2015, Tempus built one of the world's largest clinical and molecular data libraries from scratch, successfully IPO'd in June 2024, and scaled revenue from approximately $321M (2022) to $693M (2024) to an estimated $1.6B run rate in 2026 [1]. The company created entirely new categories in AI-powered diagnostics and pharma data licensing, establishing a platform that integrates genomic sequencing, clinical data, and machine learning in ways no competitor had assembled at this scale. MRD (Minimal Residual Disease) testing volume grew 500% YoY in Q1 2026, representing a transformative new capability in cancer treatment monitoring [1]. AI Deployment (2/2): AI is the core business, not a supplement. Genomic profiling algorithms, precision medicine treatment matching, and the pharma data platform are all AI-driven products sold to customers. Q1 2026 Data & Applications revenue of $87.0M (+40.5% YoY) represents directly monetized AI capabilities [1]. The Merck collaboration for AI-driven drug discovery validates the commercial AI platform at the highest level of biopharma [3]. Market Share (2/2): Tempus holds a leading position in AI-powered precision medicine, with the largest integrated clinical-molecular data library in the industry. First-mover advantage in scaled MRD testing and a growing pharma partnership portfolio anchored by the Merck collaboration demonstrate market leadership [1][3]. Competitors Foundation Medicine (Roche), Guardant Health, and Illumina each address pieces of the market, but none match Tempus's integrated data-plus-diagnostics platform breadth. Product Pipeline (1/2): The MRD testing expansion (500% volume growth), additional pharma partnerships beyond Merck, and planned expansion into cardiology and other therapeutic areas beyond oncology represent a strong pipeline [1][3]. However, execution risk remains significant given persistent GAAP losses, and the timeline for profitability in newer verticals is uncertain. R&D Intensity (1/1): Heavy R&D investment is a defining characteristic of Tempus as a healthcare AI company, with stock-based compensation and R&D spending as primary cost drivers fueling the platform's continuous expansion of capabilities.

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Eric Lefkofsky founded Tempus in 2015 after a deeply personal experience with cancer, driven by the conviction that healthcare data was fragmented and that AI could ensure every patient receives the right treatment at the right time [2]. This mission is specific, personal, and has been validated by over a decade of consistent execution building the platform — from founding through IPO to $1.27B TTM revenue. The purpose directly connects to measurable patient outcomes and has guided every strategic decision, from building the data library to launching MRD testing. The mission falls just short of a perfect score because the company has not yet survived a major crisis or existential threat that would test the depth of commitment beyond growth-phase enthusiasm. Community Impact (1/2): Precision medicine has inherent positive community impact — Tempus's genomic profiling helps oncologists select more effective treatments, and MRD testing enables earlier detection of cancer recurrence, directly improving patient outcomes [1]. However, no specific charitable programs, foundation activity, or quantified community investment beyond the commercial business itself has been identified. Employee Retention (1/2): Tempus has a dedicated Chief People Officer focused on building mission-aligned culture, and the company's growing headcount from startup to approximately 2,800 employees suggests organizational stability [2]. The founder-led environment and healthcare mission likely support retention, but no specific turnover, tenure, or employee satisfaction data is publicly available for this recently public company. Insider Buying (2/2): Lefkofsky has maintained an enormous founder ownership stake since inception, representing one of the strongest equity alignment signals among growth-stage CEOs [2]. His continued role as founder-CEO since 2015, combined with the deeply personal origin story (cancer experience), amplifies conviction far beyond typical insider ownership — this is a founder who built the company to solve a problem he personally encountered.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Tempus presents the classic growth-stage infrastructure profile: exceptional revenue growth masking deeply negative profitability and high leverage. Q1 2026 revenue of $348.1M grew 36.1% YoY, and the 5-year revenue CAGR from approximately $200M (2021) to an estimated $1.6B (2026) exceeds 50%, scoring at the maximum on both growth metrics [1]. However, every profitability metric scores at floor: D/E of 316.65% reflects significant leverage for a company still burning cash, ROE of -89.48% reflects deep unprofitability, and profit margin of -22.20% demonstrates the business has not yet achieved GAAP breakeven [4]. Forward P/E is not meaningful given negative GAAP earnings, and PEG ratio is unavailable. The adjusted EBITDA trajectory is encouraging — improving from deeply negative to -$2.8M in Q1 2026, with FY2026 guidance targeting approximately $65M positive adjusted EBITDA [1][3]. Cash of $643.8M provides runway, but the path to sustained GAAP profitability remains unproven. Liquidity floor cleared comfortably: 6.5M avg daily volume x $57.08 = $371M daily liquidity [5].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (9/9): IMMUNE. Tempus AI is an AI company — artificial intelligence and machine learning are the core product, not an operational supplement [1][2]. The company builds and deploys AI algorithms for genomic profiling, precision medicine treatment matching, drug discovery, and MRD testing. You cannot be disrupted by the technology you are building and selling. Beyond the inherent immunity of being an AI-native business, Tempus possesses structural moats that compound over time: its proprietary clinical-molecular data library — one of the world's largest — grows with every test performed, creating a flywheel where more data improves AI models, which drives more adoption, which generates more data. Healthcare AI also faces formidable regulatory barriers (CLIA certification, CAP accreditation, FDA oversight) that prevent rapid commoditization, and the deep domain expertise required to interpret genomic data in clinical context cannot be replicated by general-purpose AI companies [1][2]. AI Opportunity (1/1): 100% of Tempus's revenue is AI-driven. Q1 2026 revenue of $348.1M across both Diagnostics ($261.1M) and Data & Applications ($87.0M) segments is entirely powered by AI capabilities [1]. The Merck collaboration represents incremental AI revenue opportunity in drug discovery, and MRD testing (500% volume growth) demonstrates expanding AI applications within healthcare [1][3].

infinite_game.summary: Tempus demonstrates strong infinite-game characteristics rooted in its founder's deeply personal mission and consistent long-term execution. Lefkofsky's decision to build the platform over nearly a decade before IPO'ing, his willingness to sustain years of losses to construct the data library, and the organic expansion from diagnostics into pharma data licensing and MRD testing all reflect a company playing to keep playing rather than to win this quarter. However, as a recently public company that has not yet faced a major crisis, leadership transition, or strategic existential threat, the depth of infinite-game commitment remains unproven under pressure.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Lefkofsky's personal cancer experience birthed a genuine Just Cause — using AI to ensure every patient gets the right treatment at the right time [2]. Over 10 years of consistent execution building the platform, from founding in 2015 through the decision to delay IPO until 2024, validates this as more than marketing. The mission directly connects to measurable patient outcomes and has guided strategic decisions including the expansion into MRD testing and the Merck collaboration. However, the company has not yet survived a major crisis, leadership transition, or existential strategic threat that would test whether the cause endures beyond the founder's tenure and the current growth phase.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Tempus has a dedicated Chief People Officer focused on mission-aligned culture, and the founder-led environment provides clear organizational direction [2]. Growing from a startup to approximately 2,800 employees while maintaining a coherent mission suggests functional team dynamics. However, as a relatively young organization (founded 2015, public since 2024), there is limited public evidence of specific empowerment practices, employee satisfaction metrics, or organizational trust beyond the structural elements. No major employee controversies or turnover concerns have surfaced.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Tempus competes with Foundation Medicine (Roche), Guardant Health, and Illumina — formidable rivals each with distinct strengths [1]. Lefkofsky has acknowledged the competitive landscape and differentiated Tempus through the breadth of its integrated data platform rather than by attacking competitors' approaches. The company's strategic positioning — building the broadest clinical-molecular data library and offering both diagnostics and pharma data licensing — reflects a learning-oriented competitive mindset that leverages rivals' innovations as motivation to expand platform capabilities.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Tempus has demonstrated meaningful strategic evolution since founding — starting as a genomic data company, expanding into clinical diagnostics, then building a pharma data licensing platform (Data & Applications), and most recently launching MRD testing which grew 500% YoY [1]. Each expansion was driven by the core mission of improving patient outcomes through AI rather than by desperation or market pressure. However, none of these moves constitutes a truly transformative pivot — the evolution has been organic expansion within the healthcare AI thesis, and the company has never needed to fundamentally reinvent its business model or abandon a failing strategy.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Lefkofsky invested years and significant personal capital building Tempus while sustaining deep cash burn, choosing to stay private until 2024 to build the platform properly rather than rushing to public markets for capital [2]. He continued heavy R&D investment despite mounting losses, prioritizing long-term platform construction over near-term profitability — a decision that required genuine courage given the scale of investment required to build the world's largest clinical-molecular data library. The decision to target adjusted EBITDA breakeven in 2026 rather than cutting investment to show earlier profitability reflects continued prioritization of the mission over short-term financial metrics [1][3].

investment_thesis: Tempus AI is the leading AI-native precision medicine platform, with Q1 2026 revenue of $348.1M (+36.1% YoY) across Diagnostics ($261.1M) and Data & Applications ($87.0M), powered by one of the world's largest clinical-molecular data libraries [1]. The company's proprietary data flywheel — where every test performed improves AI models, driving more adoption and generating more data — creates a compounding competitive moat that deepens over time. MRD testing volume grew 500% YoY in Q1 2026, representing a transformative new capability in cancer treatment monitoring, while the Merck collaboration validates the pharma AI platform at the highest tier of biopharma [1][3]. Founder-CEO Lefkofsky's deeply personal mission (founded after a cancer experience) and decade-long commitment to building the platform before IPO'ing signal the strongest possible alignment between leadership conviction and shareholder interests [2]. With adjusted EBITDA improving from deeply negative to near-breakeven (-$2.8M in Q1) and FY2026 guidance targeting approximately $65M positive adjusted EBITDA, the path toward profitability is emerging [1][3]. A perfect AI Resilience score of 10 reflects the reality that Tempus IS the AI disruption — you cannot be displaced by the technology you are building.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on persistent GAAP unprofitability and extreme valuation risk. At a market cap of approximately $10.25B against TTM revenue of $1.27B with -22.2% profit margin, D/E of 316.65%, and ROE of -89.48%, the stock is priced for flawless execution of a growth story that has not yet proven it can generate sustainable bottom-line earnings [4][5]. The forward P/E is not meaningful given negative GAAP earnings, and even on an adjusted basis, the company has only just begun to approach EBITDA breakeven. Revenue growth is decelerating — from 82% (2024 to 2025) to a guided 25% (2025 to 2026) — raising questions about whether the platform can sustain hypergrowth or is normalizing toward a more conventional trajectory [3]. Competition from well-capitalized rivals (Foundation Medicine/Roche, Guardant Health, Illumina) could pressure margins as genomic testing commoditizes. Data privacy risk is a structural governance concern given the company's handling of sensitive genomic and clinical data, and any breach or regulatory action could damage the platform's trust-based business model. Lefkofsky's track record at Groupon — which peaked spectacularly then declined — may give some investors pause about long-term execution in a very different industry.
key_catalysts: Adjusted EBITDA inflection from -$2.8M in Q1 2026 to approximately $65M positive for FY2026 would validate the path to profitability and could trigger a re-rating as the company transitions from cash-burning growth story to sustainable platform [1][3]

MRD testing volume growth of 500% YoY in Q1 2026 represents a high-growth new product line that expands the total addressable market beyond initial genomic profiling into longitudinal cancer treatment monitoring [1]

Merck collaboration and potential additional pharma partnerships validate the Data & Applications segment as a scalable AI platform for drug discovery, unlocking recurring high-margin revenue from biopharma licensing [3]
key_risks: Persistent GAAP unprofitability with -22.2% profit margin, -89.48% ROE, and 316.65% D/E — the business has not yet demonstrated it can generate sustainable bottom-line earnings despite $1.27B TTM revenue, and the path from adjusted EBITDA breakeven to GAAP profitability remains long and uncertain [4][5]

Revenue growth deceleration from approximately 82% (2024-2025) to a guided 25% (2025-2026) suggests the hypergrowth phase may be normalizing, which at a $10.25B market cap leaves limited margin of safety if growth slows further or competitive pressure intensifies [3]

Genomic and clinical data privacy represents a structural governance risk — any data breach, regulatory action, or change in patient consent frameworks could undermine the trust-based foundation of the clinical-molecular data library that is the company's core competitive moat [2]
